# forced colors

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=forced%20colors%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=forced%20colors" status="Color Adjustment 1" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Adapt to high-contrast and forced-colors modes.

## Representative syntax

```css
forced-color-adjust, Canvas, CanvasText
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="forced colors" css-b64="" code-b64="" html-b64="" caption="The important part is the relationship between colors: one semantic color generates a maintainable set of states." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use forced colors in theme and state systems when you need to build color variations from semantic inputs instead of hand-tuning every state. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A warning banner can derive its background, border, and text color from one token and still adapt in dark mode.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>Perceptual spaces help with ramps, but they do not replace contrast checks against real text sizes and backgrounds.</p>
</section>
</div>

## Source metadata

- Category: Color, Theming, and High Dynamic Range
- Maturity / source: Color Adjustment 1
- Representative syntax: `forced-color-adjust, Canvas, CanvasText`
- Position in category: 12 / 15
